content:
6 min read
Just now

--

Platform threads quietly cap your concurrency. Virtual threads (Project Loom) fix the lie — without forcing you into reactive hell.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

You write clean, blocking Java.
A request comes in. You call the database. You wait. You call another service. You wait again. You return JSON. The code is readable. Your teammates love it.
Then production traffic doubles — and the server falls over while CPU sits at 20%.
That is not a mystery. That is the thread-per-request model hitting a wall it was never honest about.
For twenty years, Java servers made a quiet bet: a thread is cheap enough to spend one on every in-flight request. That bet was only sort of true. Virtual threads exist to make it actually true.
This article is Part 1 of a series. No Loom mysticism. No “just use WebFlux” sermon. Just the real difference between platform threads and virtual threads — memory, scheduling, and why your bottleneck was never the CPU.
The bet every Spring app makes
Most servlet-style servers still think like this:

• Accept a request

• Assign a thread
